title: Early Complications With Preloaded Descemet Membrane Endothelial Keratoplasty Are Not Dependent on Optisol-GS Washout or Trypan Blue Restaining.
abstract: To describe the intraoperative and early postoperative complications using preloaded Descemet membrane endothelial keratoplasty (DMEK) grafts with intraocular injection of the graft in Optisol-GS and omission of trypan blue restaining. This is a retrospective case series of 132 consecutive eyes with Fuchs endothelial dystrophy or endothelial failure who underwent DMEK using preloaded donor tissue prepared as previously described. The graft was not restained with trypan blue by the surgeon, and Optisol-GS was injected with the graft into the eye instead of being rinsed from the injector. Early postoperative complications (0-8 wk) including intraoperative fibrin formation, intraocular inflammation, elevated intraocular pressure, partial graft detachment requiring rebubble, and early graft failure were recorded. No eyes developed intraoperative fibrin formation or postoperative inflammation (such as toxic anterior segment syndrome) or elevated intraocular pressure. For eyes with Fuchs corneal dystrophy, our rebubble rate was 21% (22/106 eyes). Early graft failure was noted in 2% (3/132 eyes), which is similar to previous reports. Our results suggest that injection of Optisol-GS into the anterior chamber during DMEK graft injection does not lead to increases in intraoperative or early postoperative complications. Trypan blue restaining is not necessary for intraoperative visualization. This simplification can reduce graft manipulation and save time and resources for this procedure.
full_text:
Early Complications with Preloaded Descemet Membrane Endothelial Keratoplasty are not Dependent on Optisol-GS Washout or Trypan Blue Restaining.

Purpose:

To describe intraoperative and early postoperative complications using preloaded Descemet membrane endothelial keratoplasty (DMEK) grafts with intraocular injection of the graft in Optisol-GS and omission of trypan blue restaining.

Methods:

A retrospective case series of 132 consecutive eyes with Fuchs endothelial dystrophy or endothelial failure who underwent DMEK using preloaded donor tissue prepared as previously described. The graft was not restained with trypan blue by the surgeon and Optisol-GS was injected with the graft into the eye instead of being rinsed from the injector. Early postoperative complications (0–8 weeks) including intraoperative fibrin formation, intraocular inflammation, elevated intraocular pressure (IOP), partial graft detachment requiring re-bubble and early graft failure were recorded.

Results:

No eyes developed intraoperative fibrin formation or postoperative inflammation (such as toxic anterior segment syndrome) or elevated IOP. For eyes with Fuchs corneal dystrophy, our re-bubble rate was 21% (22/106 eyes). Early graft failure was noted in 2% (3/132 eyes), similar to previous reports.

Conclusions:

Our results suggest that injection of Optisol-GS into the anterior chamber during DMEK graft injection does not lead to increases in intraoperative or early postoperative complications. Trypan blue restaining is not necessary for intraoperative visualization. This simplification can reduce graft manipulation and save time and resources for this procedure.

Introduction:

Prestripped, prestained and preloaded Descemet membrane endothelial keratoplasty (DMEK) grafts have eased surgery difficulty and saved surgeons significant time. Initial clinical reports have shown preloaded grafts to be comparable to surgeon-loaded grafts, with similar endothelial cell loss (ECL) and similar clinical outcomes. DMEK now accounts for 43% of all endothelial keratoplasty done in the United States, up from 26% in 2017. This increase is likely due in part to availability of preloaded DMEK tissue. Between 2018 and 2019, the percentage of primary DMEK graft failures associated with preloaded processing increased from 29% to 64%. It is possible that surgical approaches with preloaded tissue may account for this increase.

It has been suggested that Optisol-GS be removed from the injector tube in which the graft is stored to reduce the risk of complications including detachments, intraocular toxicity, and inflammation. However fluid exchange may introduce bubbles and further promote endothelial cell stress or may expulse the graft out of the injector. We assessed intraoperative and early postoperative complications in 132 preloaded DMEK surgeries among 4 surgeons where Optisol-GS was not rinsed and no trypan restaining was carried out prior to injecting the graft into the eye.

Materials and Methods:

We conducted a retrospective review of the first 132 eyes that underwent prestripped, prestained and preloaded DMEK at the Casey Eye Institute in Portland, Oregon (May 16, 2017 to December 31, 2019). This study adhered to the tenets of the Declaration of Helsinki. Institutional Review Board approval was obtained at Oregon Health & Science University (OHSU). Eligibility criteria included all patients undergoing preloaded DMEK at OHSU. Surgeries were conducted by 4 experienced DMEK surgeons (AAN, JLC, RDS, and WDC). All preloaded tissue (Patient-Ready DMEK™) was supplied by the Lions VisionGift eye bank in Portland, Oregon and processed using techniques previously reported, including an “S” or “F” stamp for graft orientation. Stripped and stained grafts were stored in a Straiko-modified Jones tube (Gunther Weiss Scientific Glass Blowing Co., Inc, Portland, OR) seated in a Krolman chamber (Krolman, Boston, MA) filled with Optisol-GS (Bausch & Lomb, Bridgewater, NJ). All grafts were stored and injected in the endothelium-out configuration.

Our surgical technique is similar to others previously published. The modified Jones tube was removed from the Krolman chamber and connected to 3.0 ml syringe filled with BSS with a segment of polypropylene tubing (14 French catheter or phaco-machine outflow tubing) without introducing air bubbles. Optisol-GS was not rinsed from the injector, nor was additional trypan staining applied. The graft was injected along with the Optisol-GS into the eye. The rest of the DMEK surgery was performed as previously described, with either air or 20% sulfur hexafluoride (SF6) used, depending on patient factors and surgeon preference.

Intraoperative and early postoperative complications (0–8 weeks postoperatively) were recorded from chart review. Intraocular pressure (IOP) was checked by palpation on postoperative day 1 by the surgeon and by Tonopen (Reichert, Depew, NY) at subsequent visits. Quantity of anterior chamber cell was reported by all surgeons at postoperative week 1. Graft re-bubbles with air were conducted depending on patient factors and surgeon preference. Among the 4 surgeons included in the study, there was no strict criteria for graft rebubbles. In general, however, a rebubble was performed for a graft with greater than 20% detachment or where there was a graft detachment associated with visually significant edema or a delay in visual recovery. For the purposes of this study, primary graft failure was defined as insufficient corneal clearing or need for re-graft within the first 8 weeks postoperatively. (In slight contrast, the Eye Bank Association of America (EBAA) defines primary graft failure as the failure to clear after 8 weeks.)

A chi square test was done to evaluate differences in rebubble rates and postoperative inflammation between subsets of patients who underwent simultaneous cataract surgery and those who just had DMEK.

Results:

Preloaded DMEK was performed on 132 eyes of 103 patients. The mean patient age was 67 ±11 years. Three cases were followed outside our institution after week 1 with no reports of complications. Indications for DMEK are found in Table 1. 86% of all cases were done using air while 14% were done using 20% SF6 gas. 55% of eyes also underwent combined uncomplicated cataract surgery. The graft and its orientation was easily visualized intraoperatively in all cases without restaining.

No patients developed intraoperative fibrinous anterior chamber reaction or clinical findings consistent with toxic anterior segment syndrome (TASS) in the 8 week postoperative period. 18 % of eyes had trace/1+ cell at their postoperative week 1 visit, which resolved by their next visit. Elevated IOP occurred in 7 (5.2%) eyes: 4 on the first postoperative day resolved with partial air release from anterior chamber, 1 at postoperative day 1 in a patient with previous glaucoma resolved after restarting timolol, 1 at postoperative week 1 resolved after temporary course of timolol, and 1 at postoperative week 8 in an eye with Chandler syndrome.

Re-bubble rates for the different indications are shown in Table 2. Fuchs patients had a 21% re-bubble rate. DMEK under penetrating keratoplasty had a 63% re-bubble rate. A second graft re-bubble was required in 3 eyes. Primary graft failure was noted in 3 grafts in 2 patients (2.3%). After 1 eye failed 2 DMEK grafts and another eye failed a single DMEK graft, a Descemet stripping automated endothelial keratoplasty (DSAEK) was performed with success.

There was no difference in rebubble rates between patients that had simultaneous cataract surgery and those that did not (P=0.58). In a subanalysis of only the Fuchs patients, there was still no difference (P=0.41). With regards to postoperative inflammation at week 1, there was no difference between patients that had simultaneous cataract surgery and those that did not (P=0.08). In a subanalysis of only the Fuchs patients, there was more inflammation at postop week 1 noted in patients that underwent simultaneous cataract surgery (14/69) than in patients that did not have cataract surgery (2/36) (P=0.046).

Discussion:

We found no evidence of increased anterior segment inflammation (zero cases of intraoperative fibrin or postoperative TASS), higher rate of ocular hypertension or increased rate of other adverse graft outcomes. We adequately visualized the graft in 100% of our cases without restaining with trypan blue. Endothelial cell counts, visual acuity results and long-term graft survival are the subject of a longitudinal study.

Currently there is not an understanding about the percentage increase in preloaded DMEK primary graft failures reported by the EB Online Adverse Reaction Reporting System (OARRS) which increased over 2 fold between 2018 and 2019. Discussions at EBAA forums have suggested possible causes to include increased adoption among inexperienced surgeons, graft storage conditions, or variations in surgical technique with preloaded tissue.

Theoretical concern exists if Optisol-GS gets into the eye. Small residuals of media on the surface of PKP and DSEK grafts during corneal transplantation have not been a reported source of post-transplant problems historically. In preloaded DMEK, the volume of Optisol-GS is higher with the modified Jones tubes (~400 L which is roughly double the volume of the anterior chamber). TASS has rarely been reported in the setting of corneal transplantation. Gentamycin and streptomycin are potentially toxic but their concentrations in Optisol-GS have confirmed endothelial safety in vitro. Dextran is another component that could cause anterior chamber toxicity and if trapped in the interface could promote graft detachments.

Rinsing the graft of media and/or restaining with trypan blue prior to injection carries two risks: endothelial cell stress and accidental tissue expulsion from the glass injector. Exclusion of these steps reduces time and resources. Immediate anterior chamber dilution of Optisol-GS with small balanced salt solution (BSS) injections used to unfurl the graft and chamber flattening likely mitigates risks.

Partial graft detachments requiring 1 or more graft re-bubbles (22% total, 19% excluding DMEKs under PKP) was near the range of 14–20% found in previous preloaded studies and was well within the wide range of 2–82% reported in the literature. Our primary graft failure rate 3/132 (2.3%) is slightly less than rates observed for DSAEK 45/1330 (3.3%) in the Corneal Preservation Time Study (CPTS) and is similar to the combined rate in the Descemet Endothelial Thickness Comparison Trial (DETECT) study. We interpret these data to suggest that early complications were not a result of injecting Optisol-GS into the eye. Data on early graft failures in preloaded DMEK tissue are still emerging and may not reflect the same rates seen in surgeon-loaded tissue. Compared to some reports, our re-bubble rate may be higher. Both preloaded tissue and Optisol-GS injection are variables that would benefit from prospective studies. We noted more postoperative intraocular inflammation among the subset of only Fuchs patients who also had cataract surgery compared to Fuchs patients who just had DMEK surgery. This was not unexpected given that cataract surgery alone normally generates transient intraocular inflammation and the combination of the 2 surgeries may generate more. The retrospective, single center nature of this study may limit the generalizability of our findings. This study was not done with a negative control such as Optisol-GS washout or surgeon-loaded tissue which could have demonstrated outcome differences among our surgeons. Due to the novelty of the preloaded DMEK, we limited this report to immediate postoperative complications.

In summary, Optisol-GS washout and trypan restaining are not necessary steps in preloaded DMEK surgery to reduce intraoperative or early post-operative complications. Elimination of these steps reduces graft manipulation, time in the operating room and operating room resources. The long-term outcomes of preloaded grafts will require longitudinal studies.

Disclosures: All authors other than CGS report no relevant financial disclosures.

Christopher S. Stoeger is the chief executive officer of Lions VisionGift which patented the technology of Patient-Ready DMEK™ and currently licenses it to various other eye banks.
